2192	  relation is ``there exists an isomorphism'',
2193	  \(M \sim M' :\equiv \mathrm{Nonempty}(M \cong M')\) (a proposition-valued
2194	  equivalence relation: reflexive, symmetric and transitive because isomorphisms
2195	  compose and invert). The \emph{Picard group carrier}
2196	  \[
2197	    \Pic X \;:=\; \mathtt{Invertible}\,X \,/\!\sim
2198	  \]
2199	  is the quotient type of isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
2200	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. We write \([M] \in \Pic X\) for the class of an

2091	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2092	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible}

2095	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2096	  % modules), Lemma lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1 (an invertible module
2097	  % is locally free of rank one, when the stalks are local rings)
2098	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
2099	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2100	  % "\begin{lemma}
2101	  % \label{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
2102	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
2103	  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
2104	  % If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
2105	  % the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case).
2106	  % \end{lemma}"
2107	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M,
2108	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}: a locally free rank-one
2109	  module is invertible, and over a space whose stalks are local rings (in
2110	  particular any scheme) the converse holds, so on a scheme invertible
2111	  \(\Leftrightarrow\) locally free of rank one --- hence flat.}
2112	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
2113	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
2114	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
2115	  \[
2116	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2117	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2118	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
2119	  \]
2120	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
2121	  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
2122	  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2123	  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
2124	  coherence is ever required.
2125	\end{lemma}
2126	
2127	\begin{proof}
2128	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2129	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
2130	  On a scheme \(X\) every stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) is a local ring, so by Stacks
2131	  tag 0B8M an \(\otimes\)-invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is \emph{locally free
2132	  of rank one}, and a locally free module is in particular \emph{flat}: each
2133	  section \(M(U)\) is a flat \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module. This flatness is the key
2134	  difference from the general-module associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}.
2135	
2136	  \emph{Routing past the open obligation.} The general-module associator
2137	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is realized through the route-(d) whiskering
2138	  composite, whose left-whiskering half is the flatness-free localizer lemma
2139	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (the variants
2140	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\)).
2141	  That half is an \emph{open} obligation, so \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
2142	  currently depends on it. For \emph{invertible} \(M, N, P\) the whiskering objects are
2143	  flat, so the whiskering-stability needed by the associator is instead supplied by
2144	  the \emph{flat}-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (the variants
2145	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_flat}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_flat}\)),
2146	  which is closed: there local injectivity of \(F \triangleleft g\) follows
2147	  from \(F(U)\) flat over \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) preserving injective linear maps
2148	  (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)), with no open
2149	  residual. Restricting the construction of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to
2150	  invertible arguments therefore bypasses the flatness-free open obligation of
2151	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}.
2152	
2153	  The remaining ingredients are exactly those of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: the
2154	  three canonical arrows of the local composite are the restriction-compatibility
2155	  comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (twice, on each member of a common
2156	  open cover and in reverse) framing the canonical presheaf-level associator of
2157	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\). Each arrow is canonical, hence
2158	  natural under further restriction, so the local composites agree on double
2159	  overlaps and glue to the global associator. The invertibility hypotheses are used
2160	  only to route through the flat-whiskering lemma; the gluing itself is unchanged.
2161	\end{proof}

2100	  % "\begin{lemma}

2102	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
2103	  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.

2107	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M,
2108	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}: a locally free rank-one
2109	  module is invertible, and over a space whose stalks are local rings (in
2110	  particular any scheme) the converse holds, so on a scheme invertible
2111	  \(\Leftrightarrow\) locally free of rank one --- hence flat.}
2112	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
2113	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
2114	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
2115	  \[
2116	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2117	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2118	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
2119	  \]
2120	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
2121	  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
2122	  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2123	  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
2124	  coherence is ever required.
2125	\end{lemma}
2126	
2127	\begin{proof}
2128	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2129	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
2130	  On a scheme \(X\) every stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) is a local ring, so by Stacks
2131	  tag 0B8M an \(\otimes\)-invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is \emph{locally free
2132	  of rank one}, and a locally free module is in particular \emph{flat}: each
2133	  section \(M(U)\) is a flat \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module. This flatness is the key
2134	  difference from the general-module associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}.
2135	
2136	  \emph{Routing past the open obligation.} The general-module associator
2137	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is realized through the route-(d) whiskering
2138	  composite, whose left-whiskering half is the flatness-free localizer lemma
2139	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (the variants
2140	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\)).
2141	  That half is an \emph{open} obligation, so \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
2142	  currently depends on it. For \emph{invertible} \(M, N, P\) the whiskering objects are
2143	  flat, so the whiskering-stability needed by the associator is instead supplied by
2144	  the \emph{flat}-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (the variants
2145	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_flat}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_flat}\)),
2146	  which is closed: there local injectivity of \(F \triangleleft g\) follows
2147	  from \(F(U)\) flat over \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) preserving injective linear maps
2148	  (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)), with no open
2149	  residual. Restricting the construction of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to
2150	  invertible arguments therefore bypasses the flatness-free open obligation of
2151	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}.
2152	
2153	  The remaining ingredients are exactly those of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: the
2154	  three canonical arrows of the local composite are the restriction-compatibility
2155	  comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (twice, on each member of a common
2156	  open cover and in reverse) framing the canonical presheaf-level associator of
2157	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\). Each arrow is canonical, hence
2158	  natural under further restriction, so the local composites agree on double
2159	  overlaps and glue to the global associator. The invertibility hypotheses are used
2160	  only to route through the flat-whiskering lemma; the gluing itself is unchanged.
2161	\end{proof}

2401	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition~\texttt{definition-pic}
2402	  and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible}: the isomorphism classes of
2403	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules form an abelian group under tensor product,
2404	  with unit \(\mathcal{O}_X\).}
2405	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The carrier \(\Pic X\) of \cref{def:pic_carrier} is an
2406	  abelian group, with
2407	  \[
2408	    [M] \cdot [M'] := [M \otimes_X M'],
2409	    \qquad
2410	    1 := [\mathcal{O}_X],
2411	    \qquad
2412	    [M]^{-1} := [N] \ \text{for any witness } M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X.
2413	  \]
2414	\end{theorem}

2416	\begin{proof}
2417	  \uses{def:pic_carrier, lem:isinvertible_tensor, lem:isinvertible_unit,
2418	        lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2419	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2420	  Each group-operation datum and each axiom is supplied by a single
2421	  existence-of-isomorphism, read as an equality of classes in \(\Pic X\).
2422	
2423	  \emph{The operations are well-defined.} The product \([M] \cdot [M'] := [M
2424	  \otimes_X M']\) lands in \(\Pic X\) because \(\otimes_X\) preserves invertibility
2425	  (\cref{lem:isinvertible_tensor}), and it is independent of representatives by the
2426	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2427	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}): an isomorphism in either
2428	  argument tensors to an isomorphism, hence an equality of classes. The unit
2429	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\) lies in \(\Pic X\) by \cref{lem:isinvertible_unit}. The
2430	  inverse \([M] \mapsto [N]\), \(N\) any membership witness of
2431	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\), is well-defined by
2432	  \cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef} --- the witness is determined up to
2433	  isomorphism, so its class depends only on \([M]\). Crucially, \emph{no inverse
2434	  object is constructed}: the inverse is read off the existential carried by
2435	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\).
2436	
2437	  \emph{The axioms.} Each axiom is the image, under passage to iso-classes, of one
2438	  coherence isomorphism of \(\otimes_X\):
2439	  \[
2440	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle \lambda \rangle,
2441	    \quad
2442	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle \rho \rangle,
2443	    \quad
2444	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle \alpha \rangle,
2445	    \quad
2446	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle \beta \rangle,
2447	    \quad
2448	    \mathtt{mul\_left\_inv} \leftarrow \langle \varepsilon \rangle,
2449	  \]
2450	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity
2451	  is the invertible-case associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible},
2452	  commutativity is the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, and the left-inverse
2453	  law \([N] \cdot [M] = [N \otimes_X M] = [\mathcal{O}_X] = 1\) is the witness
2454	  isomorphism \(\varepsilon : N \otimes_X M \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) itself (obtained
2455	  from \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) by the braiding). Each axiom asserts
2456	  only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\), so no pentagon, triangle, or
2457	  hexagon coherence is invoked; commutativity makes the left-inverse law suffice
2458	  for a two-sided inverse.
2459	
2460	  This is the invertibility-carrier analogue of
2461	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, differing in exactly one respect: the
2462	  left-inverse field is discharged by the predicate's own membership witness rather
2463	  than by the manufactured dual of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. The
2464	  whole dual-infrastructure obligation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} is
2465	  thereby off this group's critical path.
2466	\end{proof}

2472	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
2473	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
2474	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2475	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
2476	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
2477	
2478	\begin{theorem}
2479	
2480	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
2481	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
2482	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
2483	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2484	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2485	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2486	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2487	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
2488	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2489	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
2490	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2491	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2492	
2493	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
2494	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
2495	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
2496	  % groups)
2497	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
2498	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2499	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
2500	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
2501	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
2502	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
2503	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
2504	  % \end{dfn}
2505	  %  [\dots]
2506	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
2507	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
2508	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
2509	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
2510	  % topologies  by
2511	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
2512	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
2513	  %  \end{dfn}"
2514	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
2515	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
2516	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
2517	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
2518	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
2519	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
2520	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
2521	  \[
2522	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
2523	  \]
2524	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
2525	  abelian-group structure with addition
2526	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2527	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
2528	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
2529	  inverse
2530	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
2531	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
2532	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2533	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
2534	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
2535	  map
2536	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
2537	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
2538	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2539	\end{theorem}
2540	
2541	\begin{proof}
2542	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2543	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2544	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2545	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2546	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2547	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2548	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
2549	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
2550	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
2551	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
2552	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
2553	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
2554	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
2555	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
2556	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
2557	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
2558	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
2559	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
2560	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
2561	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2562	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
2563	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
2564	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
2565	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
2566	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
2567	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
2568	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
2569	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
2570	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
2571	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
2572	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
2573	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
2574	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
2575	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
2576	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
2577	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
2578	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
2579	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
2580	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
2581	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
2582	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
2583	
2584	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
2585	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
2586	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
2587	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
2588	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
2589	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
2590	
2591	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
2592	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
2593	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
2594	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
2595	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
2596	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
2597	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
2598	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
2599	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
2600	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2601	\end{proof}
2602	
2603	\section{Out of scope}
2604	\label{sec:tensorobj_out_of_scope}
2605	
2606	The following are downstream of this chapter and live in dedicated
2607	sibling chapters; this chapter does \emph{not} touch them:
2608	\begin{itemize}
2609	  \item \textbf{The sheafified relative Picard functor and its abelian-group target}
2610	    (\cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification,
2611	              thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}) --- once
2612	    \cref{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj} is established, the
2613	    \(\AddCommGroup\)-valued presheaf, its \'etale sheafification, and
2614	    the sheafification preserving the abelian-group target close
2615	    against it (chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}); none of those
2616	    declarations is the responsibility of this chapter.
2617	  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) by a scheme}
2618	    (A.2.c, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) --- consumes the
2619	    full \(\AddCommGroup\)-valued sheafified presheaf;
2620	    representability is downstream of \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}.
2621	  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)} (A.3,
2622	    \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}) --- once representability is
2623	    in hand, the identity component is cut out by Hilbert polynomials;
2624	    that is independent of the tensor-product substrate of this chapter.
2625	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property} (A.4,
2626	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) --- consumes the full Route~A stack
2627	    downstream of A.3.
2628	  \item \textbf{Mathlib upstream PR of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)} ---
2629	    the substrate of this chapter is project-side, intentionally; a
2630	    parallel Mathlib upstream PR is welcome but not on the critical
2631	    path. Once the substrate ships in Mathlib, the
2632	    \texttt{TensorObjSubstrate.lean} file can be slimmed to a single
2633	    import + namespace re-export, but no downstream Lean file changes.
2634	\end{itemize}
2635	
2636	\section{Internal-consistency check}
2637	\label{sec:tensorobj_consistency_check}

2636	\section{Internal-consistency check}
2637	\label{sec:tensorobj_consistency_check}
2638	
2639	The Lean-pinned declaration blocks of this chapter form a connected
2640	\texttt{\textbackslash uses\{\dots\}} chain whose leaves all resolve
2641	either inside this chapter, inside chapter
2642	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b on disk), or inside
2643	chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c on disk):
2644	\begin{itemize}
2645	  \item \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} introduces the substrate
2646	    operation and depends on no prior block.
2647	  \item \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} uses
2648	    \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
2649	  \item \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path} uses
2650	    \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
2651	          lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:whisker_of_W}; it
2652	    records that, under route~(e), the full monoidal category is \emph{cheap}
2653	    (the output of \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\)) and that the group law still
2654	    consumes only existence-of-isomorphism propositions.
2655	  \item \cref{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal} uses
2656	    \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}; it is a self-contained
2657	    \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level supplement, off the critical path and
2658	    \emph{not} an ingredient of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- nothing
2659	    downstream depends on it.
2660	  \item \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible} (the \(\otimes\)-invertibility
2661	    predicate, carrying the inverse) uses \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
2662	  \item The route~(e) whisker lemmas
2663	    \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (local injectivity of
2664	    \(F \triangleleft g\) for \(g \in J.W\): the PRIMARY route assumes \(F\) locally
2665	    trivial and proves it sheaf-level via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and the
2666	    unitor, with no stalks; the FALLBACK route, for arbitrary \(F\), is the
2667	    stalkwise argument with residual ingredients d.1/d.2 and is the only remaining
2668	    open obligation of route~(e)) and
2669	    \cref{lem:whisker_of_W} (its closed left/right packaging) supply the two
2670	    fields of the instance \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. The latter applies Mathlib's
2671	    \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) to the already-monoidal
2672	    \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and the sheafification localizer
2673	    to give the full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
2674	    \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); it is stated-but-unformalized, pending the
2675	    sole open proof obligation.
2676	  \item The coherence isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
2677	    (left/right unitors), \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} (braiding) and
2678	    \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (associator) are supplied \emph{directly},
2679	    \emph{not} read off the route~(e) monoidal structure \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
2680	    (superseded / off-path fallback only): the unitors and braiding are the cheap
2681	    \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) of the presheaf-level coherence data, and the
2682	    associator is glued from canonical local isomorphisms through the open-immersion
2683	    restriction-compatibility iso \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. None uses
2684	    \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\), \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) or sectionwise flatness.
2685	    The associator's pinned \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) hypotheses are vestigial.
2686	  \item \cref{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W} (sheafification sends a
2687	    morphism whose underlying map is in \(J.W\) to an isomorphism) depends on no
2688	    prior block; it is a closed go/no-go bridge, retained as a supplement (the
2689	    route~(e) associator is the API-derived one, so this bridge is no longer the
2690	    associator's load-bearing step).
2691	  \item \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (left/right whiskering of a
2692	    sheafification-localizer morphism by a flat object stays in the localizer
2693	    class) uses \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}; it is a valid closed
2694	    standalone result, superseded on the critical path by the flatness-free
2695	    \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
2696	  \item \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} (the commutative group on
2697	    locally-trivial \(\otimes\)-iso-classes) uses
2698	    \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
2699	          def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2700	          lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
2701	          lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2702	          lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
2703	          lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
2704	          lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2705	          lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}. This is the group-law engine: the PRIMARY
2706	    construction is the commutative group on locally-trivial iso-classes à la
2707	    \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), needing only
2708	    \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
2709	    on top of the assembled locally-trivial associator/unitors/braiding; the
2710	    \(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\) presentation
2711	    via \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} is retained as the route~(e) fallback.
2712	  \item Under the PRIMARY locally-trivial group law, \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
2713	    (consumed by the PRIMARY proof of \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}) and
2714	    \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (in the \(\uses\) of
2715	    \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, supplying inverses) are ON the critical
2716	    path. The remaining supplements
2717	    \cref{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal,lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}

2720	  \item \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct} restricts \(\otimes\) to the

2739	\end{itemize}

2089	\begin{lemma}
2090	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on invertible (flat) objects]
2091	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2092	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2093	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2094	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
2095	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2096	  % modules), Lemma lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1 (an invertible module
2097	  % is locally free of rank one, when the stalks are local rings)
2098	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
2099	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2100	  % "\begin{lemma}
2101	  % \label{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
2102	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
2103	  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
2104	  % If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
2105	  % the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case).
2106	  % \end{lemma}"
2107	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M,
2108	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}: a locally free rank-one
2109	  module is invertible, and over a space whose stalks are local rings (in
2110	  particular any scheme) the converse holds, so on a scheme invertible
2111	  \(\Leftrightarrow\) locally free of rank one --- hence flat.}
2112	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
2113	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
2114	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
2115	  \[
2116	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2117	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2118	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
2119	  \]
2120	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
2121	  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
2122	  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2123	  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
2124	  coherence is ever required.
2125	\end{lemma}
2126	
2127	\begin{proof}
2128	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2129	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
2130	  On a scheme \(X\) every stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) is a local ring, so by Stacks
2131	  tag 0B8M an \(\otimes\)-invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is \emph{locally free
2132	  of rank one}, and a locally free module is in particular \emph{flat}: each
2133	  section \(M(U)\) is a flat \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module. This flatness is the key
2134	  difference from the general-module associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}.
2135	
2136	  \emph{Routing past the open obligation.} The general-module associator
2137	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is realized through the route-(d) whiskering
2138	  composite, whose left-whiskering half is the flatness-free localizer lemma
2139	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (the variants
2140	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\)).
2141	  That half is an \emph{open} obligation, so \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
2142	  currently depends on it. For \emph{invertible} \(M, N, P\) the whiskering objects are
2143	  flat, so the whiskering-stability needed by the associator is instead supplied by
2144	  the \emph{flat}-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (the variants
2145	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_flat}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_flat}\)),
2146	  which is closed: there local injectivity of \(F \triangleleft g\) follows
2147	  from \(F(U)\) flat over \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) preserving injective linear maps
2148	  (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)), with no open
2149	  residual. Restricting the construction of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to
2150	  invertible arguments therefore bypasses the flatness-free open obligation of
2151	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}.
2152	
2153	  The remaining ingredients are exactly those of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: the
2154	  three canonical arrows of the local composite are the restriction-compatibility
2155	  comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (twice, on each member of a common
2156	  open cover and in reverse) framing the canonical presheaf-level associator of
2157	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\). Each arrow is canonical, hence
2158	  natural under further restriction, so the local composites agree on double
2159	  overlaps and glue to the global associator. The invertibility hypotheses are used
2160	  only to route through the flat-whiskering lemma; the gluing itself is unchanged.
2161	\end{proof}

2165	  \label{def:pic_carrier}
2166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.PicGroup}
2167	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
2168	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2169	  % modules), Definition definition-pic (the Picard group as the abelian group of
2170	  % iso-classes of invertible modules under tensor product)
2171	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4350-L4357).
2172	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2173	  % "\begin{definition}
2174	  % \label{definition-pic}
2175	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2176	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
2177	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
2178	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
2179	  % corresponding to tensor product.
2180	  % \end{definition}"
2181	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition~\texttt{definition-pic}:
2182	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) is the abelian group of isomorphism classes of
2183	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, with addition the tensor product.}
2184	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. Consider the subtype of \(\otimes\)-invertible objects
2185	  \[
2186	    \mathtt{Invertible}\,X
2187	    \;:=\;
2188	    \bigl\{\, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
2189	              \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \,\bigr\}
2190	  \]
2191	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and equip it with the setoid whose
2192	  relation is ``there exists an isomorphism'',
2193	  \(M \sim M' :\equiv \mathrm{Nonempty}(M \cong M')\) (a proposition-valued
2194	  equivalence relation: reflexive, symmetric and transitive because isomorphisms
2195	  compose and invert). The \emph{Picard group carrier}
2196	  \[
2197	    \Pic X \;:=\; \mathtt{Invertible}\,X \,/\!\sim
2198	  \]
2199	  is the quotient type of isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
2200	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. We write \([M] \in \Pic X\) for the class of an
2201	  invertible \(M\). This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2202	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\), carried on \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) rather than on
2203	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\); on a scheme the two predicates agree (Stacks tag
2204	  0B8M), but only the invertibility form carries the inverse witness existentially.
2205	\end{definition}

2360	  \(N \otimes_X M \cong M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), and the outer two are
2361	  the unitors. Hence \(N \cong N'\). For well-definedness on classes, note that if
2362	  \(M \cong M'\) then a tensor inverse of \(M\) is also a tensor inverse of \(M'\)
2363	  (tensor with the isomorphism), so the inverse class depends only on \([M]\).

2369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.picCommGroup}

2385	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
2386	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
2387	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2388	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
2389	  % is an isomorphism.
2390	  % \end{enumerate}
2391	  % \end{lemma}
2392	  % [\dots]
2393	  % \begin{definition}
2394	  % \label{definition-pic}
2395	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2396	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
2397	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
2398	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
2399	  % corresponding to tensor product.
2400	  % \end{definition}"
2401	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition~\texttt{definition-pic}
2402	  and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible}: the isomorphism classes of
2403	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules form an abelian group under tensor product,
2404	  with unit \(\mathcal{O}_X\).}
2405	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The carrier \(\Pic X\) of \cref{def:pic_carrier} is an
2406	  abelian group, with
2407	  \[
2408	    [M] \cdot [M'] := [M \otimes_X M'],
2409	    \qquad

2411	    \qquad
2412	    [M]^{-1} := [N] \ \text{for any witness } M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X.
2413	  \]
2414	\end{theorem}
2415	
2416	\begin{proof}
2417	  \uses{def:pic_carrier, lem:isinvertible_tensor, lem:isinvertible_unit,
2418	        lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2419	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2420	  Each group-operation datum and each axiom is supplied by a single
2421	  existence-of-isomorphism, read as an equality of classes in \(\Pic X\).
2422	
2423	  \emph{The operations are well-defined.} The product \([M] \cdot [M'] := [M
2424	  \otimes_X M']\) lands in \(\Pic X\) because \(\otimes_X\) preserves invertibility
2425	  (\cref{lem:isinvertible_tensor}), and it is independent of representatives by the
2426	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2427	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}): an isomorphism in either
2428	  argument tensors to an isomorphism, hence an equality of classes. The unit
2429	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\) lies in \(\Pic X\) by \cref{lem:isinvertible_unit}. The
2430	  inverse \([M] \mapsto [N]\), \(N\) any membership witness of
2431	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\), is well-defined by
2432	  \cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef} --- the witness is determined up to
2433	  isomorphism, so its class depends only on \([M]\). Crucially, \emph{no inverse
2434	  object is constructed}: the inverse is read off the existential carried by
2435	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\).
2436	
2437	  \emph{The axioms.} Each axiom is the image, under passage to iso-classes, of one
2438	  coherence isomorphism of \(\otimes_X\):
2439	  \[
2440	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle \lambda \rangle,
2441	    \quad
2442	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle \rho \rangle,
2443	    \quad
2444	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle \alpha \rangle,
2445	    \quad
2446	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle \beta \rangle,
2447	    \quad
2448	    \mathtt{mul\_left\_inv} \leftarrow \langle \varepsilon \rangle,
2449	  \]
2450	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity
2451	  is the invertible-case associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible},
2452	  commutativity is the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, and the left-inverse
2453	  law \([N] \cdot [M] = [N \otimes_X M] = [\mathcal{O}_X] = 1\) is the witness
2454	  isomorphism \(\varepsilon : N \otimes_X M \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) itself (obtained
2455	  from \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) by the braiding). Each axiom asserts
2456	  only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\), so no pentagon, triangle, or
2457	  hexagon coherence is invoked; commutativity makes the left-inverse law suffice
2458	  for a two-sided inverse.
2459	
2460	  This is the invertibility-carrier analogue of
2461	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, differing in exactly one respect: the
2462	  left-inverse field is discharged by the predicate's own membership witness rather
2463	  than by the manufactured dual of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. The
2464	  whole dual-infrastructure obligation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} is
2465	  thereby off this group's critical path.
2466	\end{proof}

2468	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
2469	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
2470	
2471	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
2472	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
2473	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
2474	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2475	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
2476	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
2477	
2478	\begin{theorem}
2479	
2480	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
2481	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
2482	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
2483	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2484	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2485	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2486	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2487	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
2488	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2489	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
2490	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2491	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2492	
2493	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
2494	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
2495	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
2496	  % groups)
2497	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
2498	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2499	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
2500	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
2501	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
2502	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
2503	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
2504	  % \end{dfn}
2505	  %  [\dots]
2506	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
2507	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
2508	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
2509	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
2510	  % topologies  by
2511	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
2512	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
2513	  %  \end{dfn}"
2514	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
2515	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
2516	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
2517	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
2518	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
2519	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
2520	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
2521	  \[
2522	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
2523	  \]
2524	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
2525	  abelian-group structure with addition
2526	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2527	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
2528	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
2529	  inverse
2530	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
2531	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
2532	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2533	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
2534	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
2535	  map
2536	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
2537	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
2538	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2539	\end{theorem}
2540	
2541	\begin{proof}
2542	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2543	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2544	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2545	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2546	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2547	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2548	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
2549	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
2550	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
2551	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
2552	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
2553	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
2554	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
2555	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
2556	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
2557	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
2558	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
2559	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
2560	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
2561	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2562	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
2563	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
2564	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
2565	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
2566	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
2567	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
2568	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
2569	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
2570	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
2571	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
2572	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
2573	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
2574	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
2575	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
2576	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
2577	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
2578	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
2579	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
2580	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
2581	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
2582	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
2583	
2584	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
2585	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
2586	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
2587	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
2588	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
2589	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
2590	
2591	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
2592	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
2593	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
2594	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
2595	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
2596	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
2597	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
2598	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
2599	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
2600	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2601	\end{proof}

2081	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
2082	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
2083	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
2084	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
2085	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2086	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible

2089	\begin{lemma}
2090	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on invertible (flat) objects]
2091	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2092	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2093	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2094	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
2095	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2096	  % modules), Lemma lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1 (an invertible module
2097	  % is locally free of rank one, when the stalks are local rings)
2098	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
2099	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2100	  % "\begin{lemma}
2101	  % \label{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
2102	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
2103	  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
2104	  % If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
2105	  % the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case).
2106	  % \end{lemma}"
2107	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M,
2108	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}: a locally free rank-one
2109	  module is invertible, and over a space whose stalks are local rings (in
2110	  particular any scheme) the converse holds, so on a scheme invertible
2111	  \(\Leftrightarrow\) locally free of rank one --- hence flat.}
2112	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
2113	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
2114	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
2115	  \[
2116	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2117	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2118	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
2119	  \]
2120	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
2121	  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
2122	  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2123	  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
2124	  coherence is ever required.
2125	\end{lemma}
2126	
2127	\begin{proof}
2128	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2129	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
2130	  On a scheme \(X\) every stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) is a local ring, so by Stacks
2131	  tag 0B8M an \(\otimes\)-invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is \emph{locally free
2132	  of rank one}, and a locally free module is in particular \emph{flat}: each
2133	  section \(M(U)\) is a flat \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module. This flatness is the key
2134	  difference from the general-module associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}.
2135	
2136	  \emph{Routing past the open obligation.} The general-module associator
2137	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is realized through the route-(d) whiskering
2138	  composite, whose left-whiskering half is the flatness-free localizer lemma
2139	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (the variants
2140	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\)).
2141	  That half is an \emph{open} obligation, so \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
2142	  currently depends on it. For \emph{invertible} \(M, N, P\) the whiskering objects are
2143	  flat, so the whiskering-stability needed by the associator is instead supplied by
2144	  the \emph{flat}-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (the variants
2145	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_flat}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_flat}\)),
2146	  which is closed: there local injectivity of \(F \triangleleft g\) follows
2147	  from \(F(U)\) flat over \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) preserving injective linear maps
2148	  (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)), with no open
2149	  residual. Restricting the construction of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to
2150	  invertible arguments therefore bypasses the flatness-free open obligation of
2151	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}.
2152	
2153	  The remaining ingredients are exactly those of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: the
2154	  three canonical arrows of the local composite are the restriction-compatibility
2155	  comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (twice, on each member of a common
2156	  open cover and in reverse) framing the canonical presheaf-level associator of
2157	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\). Each arrow is canonical, hence
2158	  natural under further restriction, so the local composites agree on double
2159	  overlaps and glue to the global associator. The invertibility hypotheses are used
2160	  only to route through the flat-whiskering lemma; the gluing itself is unchanged.
2161	\end{proof}
2162	
2163	\begin{definition}
2164	[The invertibility-carrier Picard group \(\Pic X\)]
2165	  \label{def:pic_carrier}
2166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.PicGroup}
2167	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
2168	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2169	  % modules), Definition definition-pic (the Picard group as the abelian group of
2170	  % iso-classes of invertible modules under tensor product)
2171	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4350-L4357).
2172	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2173	  % "\begin{definition}
2174	  % \label{definition-pic}
2175	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2176	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
2177	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
2178	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
2179	  % corresponding to tensor product.
2180	  % \end{definition}"
2181	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition~\texttt{definition-pic}:
2182	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) is the abelian group of isomorphism classes of
2183	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, with addition the tensor product.}
2184	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. Consider the subtype of \(\otimes\)-invertible objects
2185	  \[
2186	    \mathtt{Invertible}\,X
2187	    \;:=\;
2188	    \bigl\{\, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
2189	              \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \,\bigr\}
2190	  \]
2191	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and equip it with the setoid whose
2192	  relation is ``there exists an isomorphism'',
2193	  \(M \sim M' :\equiv \mathrm{Nonempty}(M \cong M')\) (a proposition-valued
2194	  equivalence relation: reflexive, symmetric and transitive because isomorphisms
2195	  compose and invert). The \emph{Picard group carrier}
2196	  \[
2197	    \Pic X \;:=\; \mathtt{Invertible}\,X \,/\!\sim
2198	  \]
2199	  is the quotient type of isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
2200	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. We write \([M] \in \Pic X\) for the class of an
2201	  invertible \(M\). This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2202	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\), carried on \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) rather than on
2203	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\); on a scheme the two predicates agree (Stacks tag
2204	  0B8M), but only the invertibility form carries the inverse witness existentially.
2205	\end{definition}
2206	
2207	\begin{lemma}
2208	[\(\otimes\)-invertibility is closed under tensor product]
2209	  \label{lem:isinvertible_tensor}
2210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible.tensorObj}
2211	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible,
2212	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
2213	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2214	  % modules), Lemma lemma-constructions-invertible (item 1: the tensor product of

2360	  \(N \otimes_X M \cong M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), and the outer two are
2361	  the unitors. Hence \(N \cong N'\). For well-definedness on classes, note that if
2362	  \(M \cong M'\) then a tensor inverse of \(M\) is also a tensor inverse of \(M'\)
2363	  (tensor with the isomorphism), so the inverse class depends only on \([M]\).
2364	\end{proof}

2366	\begin{theorem}
2367	[The invertibility-carrier Picard group is abelian]
2368	  \label{thm:pic_commgroup}
2369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.picCommGroup}
2370	  \uses{def:pic_carrier, lem:isinvertible_tensor, lem:isinvertible_unit,
2371	        lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2372	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2373	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2374	  % modules), Definition definition-pic + Lemma lemma-constructions-invertible
2375	  % (the iso-classes of invertibles form an abelian group under tensor product)
2376	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200-L4213 and L4350-L4357).
2377	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2378	  % "\begin{lemma}
2379	  % \label{lemma-constructions-invertible}
2380	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2381	  % \begin{enumerate}
2382	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$, $\mathcal{N}$ are invertible
2383	  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, then so is
2384	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N}$.
2385	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
2386	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
2387	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2388	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
2389	  % is an isomorphism.
2390	  % \end{enumerate}
2391	  % \end{lemma}
2392	  % [\dots]
2393	  % \begin{definition}
2394	  % \label{definition-pic}
2395	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2396	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
2397	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
2398	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
2399	  % corresponding to tensor product.
2400	  % \end{definition}"
2401	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition~\texttt{definition-pic}
2402	  and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible}: the isomorphism classes of
2403	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules form an abelian group under tensor product,
2404	  with unit \(\mathcal{O}_X\).}
2405	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The carrier \(\Pic X\) of \cref{def:pic_carrier} is an
2406	  abelian group, with
2407	  \[
2408	    [M] \cdot [M'] := [M \otimes_X M'],
2409	    \qquad
2410	    1 := [\mathcal{O}_X],
2411	    \qquad
2412	    [M]^{-1} := [N] \ \text{for any witness } M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X.
2413	  \]
2414	\end{theorem}
2415	
2416	\begin{proof}
2417	  \uses{def:pic_carrier, lem:isinvertible_tensor, lem:isinvertible_unit,
2418	        lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2419	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2420	  Each group-operation datum and each axiom is supplied by a single
2421	  existence-of-isomorphism, read as an equality of classes in \(\Pic X\).
2422	
2423	  \emph{The operations are well-defined.} The product \([M] \cdot [M'] := [M
2424	  \otimes_X M']\) lands in \(\Pic X\) because \(\otimes_X\) preserves invertibility
2425	  (\cref{lem:isinvertible_tensor}), and it is independent of representatives by the
2426	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2427	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}): an isomorphism in either
2428	  argument tensors to an isomorphism, hence an equality of classes. The unit
2429	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\) lies in \(\Pic X\) by \cref{lem:isinvertible_unit}. The
2430	  inverse \([M] \mapsto [N]\), \(N\) any membership witness of
2431	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\), is well-defined by
2432	  \cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef} --- the witness is determined up to
2433	  isomorphism, so its class depends only on \([M]\). Crucially, \emph{no inverse
2434	  object is constructed}: the inverse is read off the existential carried by
2435	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\).
2436	
2437	  \emph{The axioms.} Each axiom is the image, under passage to iso-classes, of one
2438	  coherence isomorphism of \(\otimes_X\):
2439	  \[
2440	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle \lambda \rangle,
2441	    \quad
2442	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle \rho \rangle,
2443	    \quad
2444	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle \alpha \rangle,
2445	    \quad
2446	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle \beta \rangle,
2447	    \quad
2448	    \mathtt{mul\_left\_inv} \leftarrow \langle \varepsilon \rangle,
2449	  \]
2450	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity
2451	  is the invertible-case associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible},
2452	  commutativity is the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, and the left-inverse
2453	  law \([N] \cdot [M] = [N \otimes_X M] = [\mathcal{O}_X] = 1\) is the witness
2454	  isomorphism \(\varepsilon : N \otimes_X M \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) itself (obtained
2455	  from \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) by the braiding). Each axiom asserts
2456	  only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\), so no pentagon, triangle, or
2457	  hexagon coherence is invoked; commutativity makes the left-inverse law suffice
2458	  for a two-sided inverse.
2459	
2460	  This is the invertibility-carrier analogue of
2461	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, differing in exactly one respect: the
2462	  left-inverse field is discharged by the predicate's own membership witness rather
2463	  than by the manufactured dual of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. The
2464	  whole dual-infrastructure obligation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} is
2465	  thereby off this group's critical path.
2466	\end{proof}

2478	\begin{theorem}
2479	
2480	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
2481	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
2482	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
2483	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,

2492	
2493	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
2494	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
2495	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
2496	  % groups)
2497	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
2498	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2499	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
2500	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
2501	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
2502	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
2503	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
2504	  % \end{dfn}
2505	  %  [\dots]
2506	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
2507	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
2508	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
2509	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
2510	  % topologies  by
2511	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
2512	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
2513	  %  \end{dfn}"
2514	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
2515	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
2516	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
2517	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
2518	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
2519	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
2520	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
2521	  \[
2522	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
2523	  \]
2524	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
2525	  abelian-group structure with addition
2526	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2527	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
2528	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
2529	  inverse
2530	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
2531	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
2532	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2533	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
2534	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
2535	  map
2536	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
2537	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
2538	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2539	\end{theorem}
2540	
2541	\begin{proof}
2542	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2543	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2544	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2545	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2546	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2547	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2548	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
2549	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
2550	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
2551	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
2552	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
2553	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
2554	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
2555	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
2556	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
2557	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
2558	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
2559	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
2560	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
2561	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2562	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
2563	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
2564	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
2565	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
2566	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
2567	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
2568	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
2569	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
2570	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
2571	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
2572	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
2573	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
2574	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
2575	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
2576	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
2577	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
2578	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
2579	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
2580	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
2581	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
2582	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
2583	
2584	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
2585	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
2586	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
2587	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
2588	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
2589	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
2590	
2591	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
2592	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
2593	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
2594	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
2595	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
2596	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
2597	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
2598	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
2599	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
2600	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2601	\end{proof}
2602	
2603	\section{Out of scope}
2604	\label{sec:tensorobj_out_of_scope}
2605	
2606	The following are downstream of this chapter and live in dedicated
2607	sibling chapters; this chapter does \emph{not} touch them:
2608	\begin{itemize}
2609	  \item \textbf{The sheafified relative Picard functor and its abelian-group target}
2610	    (\cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification,
2611	              thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}) --- once
2612	    \cref{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj} is established, the
2613	    \(\AddCommGroup\)-valued presheaf, its \'etale sheafification, and
2614	    the sheafification preserving the abelian-group target close
2615	    against it (chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}); none of those
2616	    declarations is the responsibility of this chapter.
2617	  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) by a scheme}
2618	    (A.2.c, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) --- consumes the
2619	    full \(\AddCommGroup\)-valued sheafified presheaf;
2620	    representability is downstream of \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}.
2621	  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)} (A.3,
2622	    \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}) --- once representability is
2623	    in hand, the identity component is cut out by Hilbert polynomials;
2624	    that is independent of the tensor-product substrate of this chapter.
2625	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property} (A.4,
2626	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) --- consumes the full Route~A stack
2627	    downstream of A.3.
2628	  \item \textbf{Mathlib upstream PR of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)} ---
2629	    the substrate of this chapter is project-side, intentionally; a
2630	    parallel Mathlib upstream PR is welcome but not on the critical
2631	    path. Once the substrate ships in Mathlib, the
2632	    \texttt{TensorObjSubstrate.lean} file can be slimmed to a single
2633	    import + namespace re-export, but no downstream Lean file changes.
2634	\end{itemize}
