\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
  \textbf{Status (route mismatch, deferred).} The \emph{current} realization of this
  associator uses the route-(d) whiskering composite
  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} and the sheafification
  bridge \cref{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}); through
  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- whose left-whiskering component is
  itself unproved --- it transitively depends on an open proof obligation and is
  therefore \emph{not} axiom-clean. The gluing route described below is the
  \emph{intended} obligation-free realization, but discharging it requires
  \emph{morphism-level descent for \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)}: gluing a family of
  local isomorphisms, agreeing on overlaps, into a single global isomorphism of
  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. That is the same descent-infrastructure family
  the \(\otimes\)-inverse needs (\cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}); accordingly, the
  associator re-route \emph{and} the deletion of the vestigial whiskering/stalk
  apparatus are deferred jointly with the dual block, and are not available with the
  current infrastructure. The gluing description below records the target realization.

  The isomorphism is built by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through the
  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, with no
  recourse to any whiskering or stalk apparatus. Fix a
  common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\). On each member \(U\) form the canonical
  composite
  \[
    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
  \]
  in which:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
  \end{itemize}
  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
  composites agree on the overlap.

  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
  local inverses produces a global morphism
  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
  associator. The single non-formal input of this re-route is the
  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf
  associator (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules
  internal-hom gluing are already available.

  Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
  are not consumed; the comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is applied to the
  global, untrivialised restrictions \((M \otimes_X N)|_U\), so no free-cover
  specialisation can replace it. The vestigial
  whiskering / \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk apparatus
  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W,lem:jw_ismonoidal}) plays
  no part in this proof and is superseded. The group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
  consumes only the \emph{existence} of the resulting isomorphism, never its pentagon,
  triangle, or naturality coherence.
\end{proof}
input.new_string:
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk,
        lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
  The presheaf-level tensor product carries a \emph{canonical associator for all
  modules}, with no local-freeness, flatness or local-triviality hypothesis: the
  (symmetric) monoidal category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) supplies the natural isomorphism
  \[
    \alpha^{p}_{A,B,C} \;\colon\;
      (A \otimes^{p} B) \otimes^{p} C
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
      A \otimes^{p} (B \otimes^{p} C)
  \]
  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\)) for arbitrary
  presheaves of modules \(A, B, C\). The substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) is the
  sheafification of the presheaf tensor \(\otimes^{p}\)
  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}), and sheafification is the localization
  \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) at
  the locally-bijective class \(J.W\). The associator for \(\otimes_X\) is therefore
  obtained by \emph{transporting} \(\alpha^{p}\) across \(L\).

  This transport is governed by a single condition on the sheafification unit
  \(\eta = \mathtt{toSheafify}\). To identify \(L(A \otimes^{p} B)\) with
  \(L A \otimes_X L B\) one composes \(L(\alpha^{p})\) with the canonical comparison
  maps built from \(\eta\); these comparison maps are inverted by \(L\) precisely
  when the whiskerings of \(\eta\) by the relevant modules lie in the localizer:
  each left-whisker
  \[
    F \triangleleft \eta \;=\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes \eta,
    \qquad\text{together with its right-handed analogue,}
  \]
  must be a member of \(J.W\). This is the \emph{sole} obligation in the transport;
  no pentagon, triangle, or other coherence of \(\alpha^{p}\) is consumed, because
  what is sought is only the \emph{existence} of the transported isomorphism.

  The whiskering obligation is discharged \emph{unconditionally} by
  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}: for any module \(F\) and any
  \(g \in J.W\), the left-whisker \(F \triangleleft g\) is again in \(J.W\). Indeed a
  \(J.W\)-morphism is a stalkwise isomorphism, and by the varying-ring stalk--tensor
  commutation \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation} (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor})
  \[
    (F \triangleleft g)_x \;\cong\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
  \]
  at every point \(x\), which is an isomorphism since functors preserve isomorphisms;
  hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, so it lies in \(J.W\). No
  flatness and no local-triviality hypothesis on \(F\) enters; the right-handed
  whiskering follows by the braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure.
  Applying this to \(g = \eta\) shows the unit's whiskerings lie in \(J.W\), so the
  transport goes through and \(\alpha^{p}\) descends to an isomorphism
  \[
    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
  \]
  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\), for \emph{arbitrary}
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The locally-trivial hypotheses carried by the current
  Lean pin are not consumed.

  The group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup},
  \cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism
  --- the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong
  M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\) --- and never its pentagon, triangle, or
  naturality coherence. The flat-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
  and the open \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) whiskering route \cref{lem:whisker_of_W} are no
  longer on the critical path of this associator.
\end{proof}

tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible

\begin{lemma}
[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on invertible (flat) objects]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
  % modules), Lemma lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1 (an invertible module
  % is locally free of rank one, when the stalks are local rings)
  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{lemma}
  % \label{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
  % If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
  % the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case).
  % \end{lemma}"
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M,
  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}: a locally free rank-one
  module is invertible, and over a space whose stalks are local rings (in
  particular any scheme) the converse holds, so on a scheme invertible
  \(\Leftrightarrow\) locally free of rank one --- hence flat.}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
  \[
    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
  \]
  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
  coherence is ever required.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
  On a scheme \(X\) every stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) is a local ring, so by Stacks
  tag 0B8M an \(\otimes\)-invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is \emph{locally free
  of rank one}, and a locally free module is in particular \emph{flat}: each
  section \(M(U)\) is a flat \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module. This flatness is the key
  difference from the general-module associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}.

  \emph{Routing past the open obligation.} The general-module associator
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is realized through the route-(d) whiskering
  composite, whose left-whiskering half is the flatness-free localizer lemma
  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (the variants
  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\)).
  That half is an \emph{open} obligation, so \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
  currently depends on it. For \emph{invertible} \(M, N, P\) the whiskering objects are
  flat, so the whiskering-stability needed by the associator is instead supplied by
  the \emph{flat}-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (the variants
  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_flat}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_flat}\)),
  which is closed: there local injectivity of \(F \triangleleft g\) follows
  from \(F(U)\) flat over \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) preserving injective linear maps
  (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)), with no open
  residual. Restricting the construction of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to
  invertible arguments therefore bypasses the flatness-free open obligation of
  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}.

  The remaining ingredients are exactly those of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: the
  three canonical arrows of the local composite are the restriction-compatibility
  comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (twice, on each member of a common
  open cover and in reverse) framing the canonical presheaf-level associator of
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\). Each arrow is canonical, hence
  natural under further restriction, so the local composites agree on double
  overlaps and glue to the global associator. The invertibility hypotheses are used
  only to route through the flat-whiskering lemma; the gluing itself is unchanged.
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}
[The invertibility-carrier Picard group \(\Pic X\)]
  \label{def:pic_carrier}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.PicGroup}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
  % modules), Definition definition-pic (the Picard group as the abelian group of
  % iso-classes of invertible modules under tensor product)
  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4350-L4357).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{definition}
  % \label{definition-pic}
  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
  % corresponding to tensor product.
  % \end{definition}"
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition~\texttt{definition-pic}:
  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) is the abelian group of isomorphism classes of
  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, with addition the tensor product.}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme. Consider the subtype of \(\otimes\)-invertible objects
  \[
    \mathtt{Invertible}\,X
    \;:=\;
    \bigl\{\, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
              \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \,\bigr\}
  \]
  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and equip it with the setoid whose
  relation is ``there exists an isomorphism'',
  \(M \sim M' :\equiv \mathrm{Nonempty}(M \cong M')\) (a proposition-valued
  equivalence relation: reflexive, symmetric and transitive because isomorphisms
  compose and invert). The \emph{Picard group carrier}
  \[
    \Pic X \;:=\; \mathtt{Invertible}\,X \,/\!\sim
  \]
  is the quotient type of isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. We write \([M] \in \Pic X\) for the class of an
  invertible \(M\). This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\), carried on \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) rather than on
  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\); on a scheme the two predicates agree (Stacks tag
  0B8M), but only the invertibility form carries the inverse witness existentially.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[\(\otimes\)-invertibility is closed under tensor product]
  \label{lem:isinvertible_tensor}

## Slug
assoc-d2

## Status
IN PROGRESS — edits being applied; final status to be written once all four
tasks land. (An earlier draft of this report wrongly diagnosed a permanent tool
failure; the tool channel is actually working, just heavily delayed/buffered.)

blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex

  critical path" (it IS formalized; off-path because the associator now goes via

- Task 2a: `lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso` statement `\uses` repointed to
  `{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk,
  lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}`; proof fully rewritten to the unconditional
  presheaf-associator + sheafification-transport route closed by the d.2 lemma.
- `% archon:covers` extended with `.../TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean`.

## Remaining
- Task 2b: demote `lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible` to a corollary; drop
  `lem:flat_whisker_localizer` from its `\uses`.
- Task 3: new `\section{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}` with
  `lem:stalk_tensor_commutation` + `lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk`.

17	\section{Introduction}
18	\label{section-introduction}
19	
20	\noindent
21	In this chapter we work out basic notions of sheaves of modules.
22	This in particular includes the case of abelian sheaves, since
23	these may be viewed as sheaves of $\underline{\mathbf{Z}}$-modules.
24	Basic references are \cite{FAC}, \cite{EGA} and \cite{SGA4}.
25	
26	\medskip\noindent
27	We work out what happens for sheaves of modules on ringed topoi
28	in another chapter (see
29	Modules on Sites, Section \ref{sites-modules-section-introduction}),
30	although there we will mostly just duplicate the discussion
31	from this chapter.
32	
33	
34	
35	
36	
37	\section{Pathology}
38	\label{section-pathology}
39	
40	\noindent
41	A ringed space is a pair consisting of a topological space $X$
42	and a sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}$. We allow $\mathcal{O} = 0$
43	in the definition. In this case the category of modules has a
44	single object (namely $0$). It is still an abelian category etc,
45	but it is a little degenerate. Similarly the sheaf $\mathcal{O}$
46	may be zero over open subsets of $X$, etc.
47	
48	\medskip\noindent
49	This doesn't happen when considering locally ringed spaces (as we
50	will do later).
51	
52	
53	
54	
55	
56	
57	
58	
59	\section{The abelian category of sheaves of modules}
60	\label{section-kernels}
61	
62	\noindent
63	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space, see
64	Sheaves, Definition \ref{sheaves-definition-ringed-space}.
65	Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, see
66	Sheaves, Definition \ref{sheaves-definition-sheaf-modules}.
67	Let $\varphi, \psi : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}$
68	be morphisms of sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
69	We define $\varphi + \psi : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}$
70	to be the map which on each open $U \subset X$ is the
71	sum of the maps induced by $\varphi$, $\psi$. This is
72	clearly again a map of sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
73	It is also clear that composition of maps of
74	$\mathcal{O}_X$-modules is bilinear with respect to this
75	addition. Thus $\textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$ is a pre-additive
76	category, see Homology, Definition \ref{homology-definition-preadditive}.
77	
78	\medskip\noindent
79	We will denote $0$ the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
80	which has constant value $\{0\}$ for all open $U \subset X$.
81	Clearly this is both a final and an initial object of
82	$\textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$. Given a morphism
83	of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\varphi : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}$
84	the following are equivalent:
85	(a) $\varphi$ is zero, (b) $\varphi$ factors through $0$,
86	(c) $\varphi$ is zero on sections over each open $U$, and
87	(d) $\varphi_x = 0$ for all $x \in X$. See
88	Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-points-exactness}.
89	
90	\medskip\noindent
91	Moreover, given a pair
92	$\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ of sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
93	we may define the direct sum as
94	$$
95	\mathcal{F} \oplus \mathcal{G} = \mathcal{F} \times \mathcal{G}
96	$$
97	with obvious maps $(i, j, p, q)$ as in Homology, Definition
98	\ref{homology-definition-direct-sum}. Thus $\textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
99	is an additive category, see
100	Homology, Definition \ref{homology-definition-additive-category}.
101	
102	\medskip\noindent
103	Let $\varphi : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}$ be a morphism
104	of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules. We may define $\Ker(\varphi)$
105	to be the subsheaf of $\mathcal{F}$ with sections
106	$$
107	\Ker(\varphi)(U) =
108	\{ s \in \mathcal{F}(U) \mid \varphi(s) = 0 \text{ in } \mathcal{G}(U)\}
109	$$
110	for all open $U \subset X$. It is easy to see that this is indeed
111	a kernel in the category of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules. In other words,
112	a morphism $\alpha : \mathcal{H} \to \mathcal{F}$ factors
113	through $\Ker(\varphi)$ if and only if $\varphi \circ \alpha = 0$.
114	Moreover, on the level of stalks we have
115	$\Ker(\varphi)_x = \Ker(\varphi_x)$.
116	
117	\medskip\noindent
118	On the other hand, we define
119	$\Coker(\varphi)$ as the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
120	associated to the presheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules defined
121	by the rule
122	$$
123	U
124	\longmapsto
125	\Coker(\mathcal{F}(U)\to \mathcal{G}(U)) =
126	\mathcal{G}(U)/\varphi(\mathcal{F}(U)).
127	$$
128	Since taking stalks commutes with taking sheafification, see
129	Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-stalk-sheafification} we
130	see that $\Coker(\varphi)_x = \Coker(\varphi_x)$.
131	Thus the map $\mathcal{G} \to \Coker(\varphi)$ is surjective
132	(as a map of sheaves of sets),
133	see Sheaves, Section \ref{sheaves-section-exactness-points}.
134	To show that this is a cokernel, note that if
135	$\beta : \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H}$ is a morphism of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
136	such that $\beta \circ \varphi$ is zero, then you get for every
137	open $U \subset X$ a map induced by $\beta$ from
138	$\mathcal{G}(U)/\varphi(\mathcal{F}(U))$ into $\mathcal{H}(U)$.
139	By the universal property of sheafification (see
140	Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-sheafification-presheaf-modules})
141	we obtain a canonical map $\Coker(\varphi) \to \mathcal{H}$
142	such that the original $\beta$ is equal to
143	the composition
144	$\mathcal{G} \to \Coker(\varphi) \to \mathcal{H}$.
145	The morphism $\Coker(\varphi) \to \mathcal{H}$ is unique
146	because of the surjectivity mentioned above.
147	
148	\begin{lemma}
149	\label{lemma-abelian}
150	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. The category
151	$\textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$ is an abelian category. Moreover
152	a complex
153	$$
154	\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H}
155	$$
156	is exact at $\mathcal{G}$ if and only if for all $x \in X$ the
157	complex
158	$$
159	\mathcal{F}_x \to \mathcal{G}_x \to \mathcal{H}_x
160	$$
161	is exact at $\mathcal{G}_x$.
162	\end{lemma}
163	
164	\begin{proof}
165	By Homology, Definition \ref{homology-definition-abelian-category}
166	we have to show that image and coimage agree. By Sheaves,
167	Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-points-exactness} it is enough to show
168	that image and coimage have the same stalk at every $x \in X$.
169	By the constructions of kernels and cokernels above these stalks
170	are the coimage and image in the categories of $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-modules.
171	Thus we get the result from the fact that the category of modules
172	over a ring is abelian.
173	\end{proof}
174	
175	\noindent
176	Actually the category $\textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$ has many more properties.
177	Here are two constructions we can do.
178	\begin{enumerate}
179	\item Given any set $I$ and for each $i \in I$ a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
180	we can form the product
181	$$
182	\prod\nolimits_{i \in I} \mathcal{F}_i
183	$$
184	which is the sheaf that associates to each open $U$ the
185	product of the modules $\mathcal{F}_i(U)$. This is also the
186	categorical product, as in
187	Categories, Definition \ref{categories-definition-product}.
188	\item Given any set $I$ and for each $i \in I$ a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
189	we can form the direct sum
190	$$
191	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i \in I} \mathcal{F}_i
192	$$
193	which is the {\it sheafification} of the presheaf
194	that associates to each open $U$ the
195	direct sum of the modules $\mathcal{F}_i(U)$.
196	This is also the categorical coproduct, as in
197	Categories, Definition \ref{categories-definition-coproduct}.
198	To see this you use the universal property of sheafification.
199	\end{enumerate}
200	Using these we conclude that all limits and colimits exist in
201	$\textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$.
202	
203	\begin{lemma}
204	\label{lemma-limits-colimits}
205	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
206	\begin{enumerate}
207	\item All limits exist in $\textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$.
208	Limits are the same as the corresponding limits of presheaves of
209	$\mathcal{O}_X$-modules (i.e., commute with taking
210	sections over opens).
211	\item All colimits exist in $\textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$.
212	Colimits are the sheafification of the corresponding colimit in
213	the category of presheaves. Taking colimits commutes with taking
214	stalks.
215	\item Filtered colimits are exact.
216	\item Finite direct sums are the same as the corresponding
217	finite direct sums of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
218	\end{enumerate}
219	\end{lemma}
220	
221	\begin{proof}
222	As $\textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$ is abelian (Lemma \ref{lemma-abelian})
223	it has all finite limits and colimits
224	(Homology, Lemma \ref{homology-lemma-colimit-abelian-category}).
225	Thus the existence of limits and colimits and their description follows from
226	the existence of products and coproducts and their description
227	(see discussion above) and
228	Categories, Lemmas \ref{categories-lemma-limits-products-equalizers} and
229	\ref{categories-lemma-colimits-coproducts-coequalizers}.
230	Since sheafification commutes with taking stalks we see that
231	colimits commute with taking stalks. Part (3) signifies that given
232	a system $0 \to \mathcal{F}_i \to \mathcal{G}_i \to \mathcal{H}_i \to 0$
233	of exact sequences of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules over a directed set $I$
234	the sequence $0 \to \colim \mathcal{F}_i \to \colim \mathcal{G}_i \to
235	\colim \mathcal{H}_i \to 0$ is exact as well. Since we can check
236	exactness on stalks (Lemma \ref{lemma-abelian}) this follows from the case
237	of modules which is
238	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-directed-colimit-exact}.
239	We omit the proof of (4).
240	\end{proof}
241	
242	\noindent
243	The existence of limits and colimits
244	allows us to consider exactness properties of
245	functors defined on the category of $\mathcal{O}$-modules
246	in terms of limits and colimits, as in
247	Categories, Section \ref{categories-section-exact-functor}.
248	See Homology, Lemma \ref{homology-lemma-exact-functor} for a
249	description of exactness
250	properties in terms of short exact sequences.
251	
252	\begin{lemma}
253	\label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
254	Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
255	be a morphism of ringed spaces.
256	\begin{enumerate}
257	\item The functor
258	$f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
259	is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
260	\item The functor
261	$f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
262	is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
263	\item Pullback $f^{-1} : \textit{Ab}(Y) \to \textit{Ab}(X)$
264	on abelian sheaves is exact.
265	\end{enumerate}
266	\end{lemma}
267	
268	\begin{proof}
269	Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
270	of functors, see
271	Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}
272	and
273	Categories, Section \ref{categories-section-adjoint}.
274	Part (3) holds because exactness can be checked on stalks
275	(Lemma \ref{lemma-abelian})
276	and the description of stalks of the pullback, see
277	Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-pullback-abelian-stalk}.
278	\end{proof}
279	
280	\begin{lemma}
281	\label{lemma-j-shriek-exact}
282	Let $j : U \to X$ be an open immersion of topological spaces.
283	The functor $j_! : \textit{Ab}(U) \to \textit{Ab}(X)$
284	is exact.
285	\end{lemma}
286	
287	\begin{proof}
288	Follows from the description of stalks
289	given in Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-j-shriek-abelian}.
290	\end{proof}
291	
292	\begin{lemma}
293	\label{lemma-section-direct-sum-quasi-compact}
294	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
295	Let $I$ be a set. For $i \in I$, let $\mathcal{F}_i$
296	be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
297	For $U \subset X$ quasi-compact open the map
298	$$
299	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i \in I} \mathcal{F}_i(U)
300	\longrightarrow
301	\left(\bigoplus\nolimits_{i \in I} \mathcal{F}_i\right)(U)
302	$$
303	is bijective.
304	\end{lemma}
305	
306	\begin{proof}
307	If $s$ is an element of the right hand side, then
308	there exists an open covering $U = \bigcup_{j \in J} U_j$
309	such that $s|_{U_j}$ is a finite sum
310	$\sum_{i \in I_j} s_{ji}$ with $s_{ji} \in \mathcal{F}_i(U_j)$.
311	Because $U$ is quasi-compact we may assume that the
312	covering is finite, i.e., that $J$ is finite.
313	Then $I' = \bigcup_{j \in J} I_j$ is a finite subset of
314	$I$. Clearly, $s$ is a section of the subsheaf
315	$\bigoplus_{i \in I'} \mathcal{F}_i$. The result follows
316	from the fact that for a finite direct sum sheafification
317	is not needed, see Lemma \ref{lemma-limits-colimits} above.
318	\end{proof}
319	
320	
321	
322	\section{Sections of sheaves of modules}
323	\label{section-sections}
324	
325	\noindent
326	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
327	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
328	Let $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) = \mathcal{F}(X)$ be a
329	global section. There is a unique {\it map of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules}
330	$$
331	\mathcal{O}_X \longrightarrow \mathcal{F}, \ f \longmapsto fs
332	$$
333	{\it associated to $s$}. The notation above signifies that a local
334	section $f$ of $\mathcal{O}_X$, i.e., a section $f$ over some open $U$,
335	is mapped to the multiplication of $f$ with the restriction of $s$ to
336	$U$. Conversely, any map $\varphi : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{F}$
337	gives rise to a section $s = \varphi(1)$ such that $\varphi$ is
338	the morphism associated to $s$.
339	
340	\begin{definition}
341	\label{definition-globally-generated}
342	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
343	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
344	We say that $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it generated by global
345	sections} if there exist a set $I$, and
346	global sections $s_i \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})$, $i \in I$
347	such that the map
348	$$
349	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i \in I}
350	\mathcal{O}_X \longrightarrow \mathcal{F}
351	$$
352	which is the map associated to $s_i$ on the summand corresponding to $i$,
353	is surjective. In this case we say that the sections $s_i$
354	{\it generate} $\mathcal{F}$.
355	\end{definition}
356	
357	\noindent
358	We often use the abuse of notation introduced in
359	Sheaves, Section \ref{sheaves-section-stalks} where, given a local
360	section $s$ of $\mathcal{F}$ defined in an open neighbourhood
361	of a point $x \in X$, we denote $s_x$, or even $s$ the image of $s$
362	in the stalk $\mathcal{F}_x$.
363	
364	\begin{lemma}
365	\label{lemma-globally-generated}
366	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
367	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
368	Let $I$ be a set. Let
369	$s_i \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})$, $i \in I$
370	be global sections. The sections $s_i$ generate
371	$\mathcal{F}$ if and only if for all $x\in X$ the
372	elements $s_{i, x} \in \mathcal{F}_x$ generate
373	the $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-module $\mathcal{F}_x$.
374	\end{lemma}
375	
376	\begin{proof}
377	Omitted.
378	\end{proof}
379	
380	\begin{lemma}
381	\label{lemma-tensor-product-globally-generated}
382	\begin{slogan}
383	The tensor product of globally generated sheaves of modules is
384	globally generated.
385	\end{slogan}
386	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
387	Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
388	If $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ are generated by global sections
389	then so is $\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}$.
390	\end{lemma}
391	
392	\begin{proof}
393	Omitted.
394	\end{proof}
395	
396	\begin{lemma}
397	\label{lemma-generated-by-local-sections}
398	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
399	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
400	Let $I$ be a set. Let $s_i$, $i \in I$ be a collection
401	of local sections of $\mathcal{F}$, i.e., $s_i \in \mathcal{F}(U_i)$
402	for some opens $U_i \subset X$. There exists a unique smallest
403	subsheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{G}$ such
404	that each $s_i$ corresponds to a local section of
405	$\mathcal{G}$.
406	\end{lemma}
407	
408	\begin{proof}
409	Consider the subpresheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
410	defined by the rule
411	$$
412	U
413	\longmapsto
414	\{
415	\text{sums } \sum\nolimits_{i \in J} f_i (s_i|_U)
416	\text{ where } J \text{ is finite, }
417	U \subset U_i \text{ for } i\in J, \text{ and }
418	f_i \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)
419	\}
420	$$
421	Let $\mathcal{G}$ be the sheafification of this subpresheaf.
422	This is a subsheaf of $\mathcal{F}$ by
423	Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-sheafify-epi-mono}.
424	Since all the finite sums clearly have to be in $\mathcal{G}$
425	this is the smallest subsheaf as desired.
426	\end{proof}
427	
428	\begin{definition}
429	\label{definition-generated-by-local-sections}
430	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
431	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
432	Given a set $I$, and
433	local sections $s_i$, $i \in I$ of $\mathcal{F}$
434	we say that the subsheaf $\mathcal{G}$ of
435	Lemma \ref{lemma-generated-by-local-sections}
436	above is the {\it subsheaf generated by the $s_i$}.
437	\end{definition}
438	
439	\begin{lemma}
440	\label{lemma-generated-by-local-sections-stalk}
441	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
442	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
443	Given a set $I$, and
444	local sections $s_i$, $i \in I$ of $\mathcal{F}$.
445	Let $\mathcal{G}$ be the subsheaf generated by the
446	$s_i$ and let $x\in X$.
447	Then $\mathcal{G}_x$ is the $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-submodule of
448	$\mathcal{F}_x$ generated by the elements $s_{i, x}$
449	for those $i$ such that $s_i$ is defined at $x$.
450	\end{lemma}
451	
452	\begin{proof}
453	This is clear from the construction of $\mathcal{G}$
454	in the proof of Lemma \ref{lemma-generated-by-local-sections}.
455	\end{proof}

466	\section{Supports of modules and sections}
467	\label{section-support}
468	
469	\begin{definition}
470	\label{definition-support}
471	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
472	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
473	\begin{enumerate}
474	\item The {\it support of $\mathcal{F}$} is the set of
475	points $x \in X$ such that $\mathcal{F}_x \not = 0$.
476	\item We denote $\text{Supp}(\mathcal{F})$ the support of $\mathcal{F}$.
477	\item Let $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})$ be a global section.
478	The {\it support of $s$} is the set of points $x \in X$
479	such that the image $s_x \in \mathcal{F}_x$ of $s$ is
480	not zero.
481	\end{enumerate}
482	\end{definition}
483	
484	\noindent
485	Of course the support of a local section is then defined also
486	since a local section is a global section of the restriction of
487	$\mathcal{F}$.
488	
489	\begin{lemma}
490	\label{lemma-support-section-closed}
491	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
492	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
493	Let $U \subset X$ open.
494	\begin{enumerate}
495	\item The support of $s \in \mathcal{F}(U)$ is closed in $U$.
496	\item The support of $fs$ is contained in the intersections
497	of the supports of $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ and $s \in \mathcal{F}(U)$.
498	\item The support of $s + s'$ is contained in the union of
499	the supports of $s, s' \in \mathcal{F}(U)$.
500	\item The support of $\mathcal{F}$ is the union of the supports
501	of all local sections of $\mathcal{F}$.
502	\item If $\varphi : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}$ is a morphism of
503	$\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, then the support of $\varphi(s)$ is
504	contained in the support of $s \in \mathcal{F}(U)$.
505	\end{enumerate}
506	\end{lemma}
507	
508	\begin{proof}
509	This is true because if $s_x = 0$, then $s$ is zero
510	in an open neighbourhood of $x$ by definition of stalks.
511	Similarly for $f$. Details omitted.
512	\end{proof}
513	
514	\noindent
515	In general the support of a sheaf of modules is not closed.
516	Namely, the sheaf could be an abelian sheaf on $\mathbf{R}$
517	(with the usual archimedean topology)
518	which is the direct sum of infinitely many nonzero skyscraper
519	sheaves each supported at a single point $p_i$ of $\mathbf{R}$.
520	Then the support would be the set of points $p_i$
521	which may not be closed.
522	
523	\medskip\noindent
524	Another example is to consider the open immersion
525	$j : U = (0 , \infty) \to \mathbf{R} = X$, and the abelian sheaf
526	$j_!\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_U$. By Sheaves, Section
527	\ref{sheaves-section-open-immersions} the support of
528	this sheaf is exactly $U$.
529	
530	\begin{lemma}
531	\label{lemma-support-sheaf-rings-closed}
532	Let $X$ be a topological space.
533	The support of a sheaf of rings is closed.
534	\end{lemma}
535	
536	\begin{proof}
537	This is true because (according to our conventions)
538	a ring is $0$ if and only if
539	$1 = 0$, and hence the support of a sheaf of rings
540	is the support of the unit section.
541	\end{proof}
542

548	\section{Closed immersions and abelian sheaves}
549	\label{section-closed-immersions}
550	
551	\noindent
552	Recall that we think of an abelian sheaf on a topological space $X$ as a
553	sheaf of $\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_X$-modules. Thus we may apply any results,
554	definitions for sheaves of modules to abelian sheaves.
555	
556	\begin{lemma}
557	\label{lemma-i-star-exact}
558	Let $X$ be a topological space. Let $Z \subset X$ be a closed subset.
559	Denote $i : Z \to X$ the inclusion map. The functor
560	$$
561	i_* : \textit{Ab}(Z) \longrightarrow \textit{Ab}(X)
562	$$
563	is exact, fully faithful, with essential image exactly those
564	abelian sheaves whose support is contained in $Z$. The functor $i^{-1}$
565	is a left inverse to $i_*$.
566	\end{lemma}
567	
568	\begin{proof}
569	Exactness follows from the description of
570	stalks in Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-stalks-closed-pushforward}
571	and Lemma \ref{lemma-abelian}. The rest was shown in
572	Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-equivalence-categories-closed-abelian}.
573	\end{proof}
574	
575	\noindent
576	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be an abelian sheaf on the topological space $X$. Given
577	a closed subset $Z$, there is a canonical abelian subsheaf of $\mathcal{F}$
578	which consists of exactly those sections whose support is contained in $Z$.
579	Here is the exact statement.
580	
581	\begin{remark}
582	\label{remark-sections-support-in-closed}
583	Let $X$ be a topological space. Let $Z \subset X$ be a closed subset.
584	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be an abelian sheaf on $X$. For $U \subset X$ open set, define
585	$$
586	\mathcal{H}_Z(\mathcal{F})(U) =
587	\{s \in \mathcal{F}(U) \mid
588	\text{ the support of }s\text{ is contained in }Z \cap U\}.
589	$$
590	Then $\mathcal{H}_Z(\mathcal{F})$ is an abelian subsheaf of $\mathcal{F}$.
591	It is the largest abelian subsheaf of $\mathcal{F}$ whose support is
592	contained in $Z$. By Lemma \ref{lemma-i-star-exact} we may (and we do)
593	view $\mathcal{H}_Z(\mathcal{F})$ as an abelian sheaf on $Z$.
594	In this way we obtain a left exact functor
595	$$
596	\textit{Ab}(X) \longrightarrow \textit{Ab}(Z),\quad
597	\mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{H}_Z(\mathcal{F})
598	\text{ viewed as abelian sheaf on }Z
599	$$
600	All of the statements made above follow directly from
601	Lemma \ref{lemma-support-section-closed}.
602	\end{remark}
603	
604	\noindent
605	This seems like a good opportunity to show that the functor
606	$i_*$ has a right adjoint on abelian sheaves.
607	
608	\begin{lemma}
609	\label{lemma-i-star-right-adjoint}
610	Let $i : Z \to X$ be the inclusion of a closed subset into the
611	topological space $X$. The functor $\textit{Ab}(X) \to \textit{Ab}(Z)$,
612	$\mathcal{F} \mapsto \mathcal{H}_Z(\mathcal{F})$ of
613	Remark \ref{remark-sections-support-in-closed}
614	is a right adjoint to $i_* : \textit{Ab}(Z) \to \textit{Ab}(X)$.
615	In particular $i_*$ commutes with arbitrary colimits.
616	\end{lemma}
617	
618	\begin{proof}
619	We have to show that for any abelian sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$ and any
620	abelian sheaf $\mathcal{G}$ on $Z$ we have
621	$$
622	\Hom_{\textit{Ab}(X)}(i_*\mathcal{G}, \mathcal{F}) =
623	\Hom_{\textit{Ab}(Z)}(\mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}_Z(\mathcal{F}))
624	$$
625	This is clear because after all any section of $i_*\mathcal{G}$
626	has support in $Z$. Details omitted.
627	\end{proof}
628	
629	\begin{remark}
630	\label{remark-i-star-right-adjoint}
631	In Sheaves, Remark \ref{sheaves-remark-i-star-not-exact}
632	we showed that $i_*$ as a functor
633	on the categories of sheaves of sets
634	does not have a right adjoint simply because
635	it is not exact. However, it is very close to being
636	true, in fact, the functor $i_*$ is exact on sheaves
637	of pointed sets, sections with support in $Z$ can
638	be defined for sheaves of pointed sets, and $\mathcal{H}_Z$
639	makes sense and is a right adjoint to $i_*$.
640	\end{remark}

650	\section{A canonical exact sequence}
651	\label{section-canonical-exact-sequence}
652	
653	\noindent
654	We give this exact sequence its own section.
655	
656	\begin{lemma}
657	\label{lemma-canonical-exact-sequence}
658	Let $X$ be a topological space.
659	Let $U \subset X$ be an open subset with complement $Z \subset X$.
660	Denote $j : U \to X$ the open immersion and
661	$i : Z \to X$ the closed immersion.
662	For any sheaf of abelian groups $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$
663	the adjunction mappings $j_{!}j^{-1}\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{F}$ and
664	$\mathcal{F} \to i_*i^{-1}\mathcal{F}$ give a short exact
665	sequence
666	$$
667	0 \to j_{!}j^{-1}\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{F} \to i_*i^{-1}\mathcal{F} \to 0
668	$$
669	of sheaves of abelian groups. For any morphism
670	$\varphi : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}$ of abelian sheaves on $X$
671	we obtain a morphism of short exact sequences
672	$$
673	\xymatrix{
674	0 \ar[r] &
675	j_{!}j^{-1}\mathcal{F} \ar[r] \ar[d] &
676	\mathcal{F} \ar[r] \ar[d] &
677	i_*i^{-1}\mathcal{F} \ar[r] \ar[d] &
678	0 \\
679	0 \ar[r] &
680	j_{!}j^{-1}\mathcal{G} \ar[r] &
681	\mathcal{G} \ar[r] &
682	i_*i^{-1}\mathcal{G} \ar[r] &
683	0
684	}
685	$$
686	\end{lemma}
687	
688	\begin{proof}
689	The functoriality of the short exact sequence is
690	immediate from the naturality of the adjunction mappings.
691	We may check exactness on stalks (Lemma \ref{lemma-abelian}).
692	For a description of the stalks in question see
693	Sheaves, Lemmas \ref{sheaves-lemma-j-shriek-abelian}
694	and \ref{sheaves-lemma-stalks-closed-pushforward}.
695	\end{proof}

704	\section{Modules locally generated by sections}
705	\label{section-locally-generated}
706	
707	\noindent
708	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
709	In this and the following section we will often restrict
710	sheaves to open subspaces $U \subset X$, see
711	Sheaves, Section \ref{sheaves-section-open-immersions}.
712	In particular, we will often denote the open subspace
713	by $(U, \mathcal{O}_U)$ instead of the more correct
714	notation $(U, \mathcal{O}_X|_U)$, see
715	Sheaves, Definition \ref{sheaves-definition-restriction}.
716	
717	\medskip\noindent
718	Consider the open immersion
719	$j : U = (0 , \infty) \to \mathbf{R} = X$, and the abelian sheaf
720	$j_!\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_U$. By Sheaves, Section
721	\ref{sheaves-section-open-immersions} the stalk of
722	$j_!\underline{\mathbf{Z}}_U$ at $x = 0$ is $0$. In fact the
723	sections of this sheaf over any open interval containing $0$
724	are $0$. Thus there is no open neighbourhood of the point
725	$0$ over which the sheaf can be generated by sections.
726	
727	\begin{definition}
728	\label{definition-locally-generated}
729	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
730	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
731	We say that $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it locally generated by sections}
732	if for every $x \in X$ there exists an open
733	neighbourhood $U$ of $x$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$
734	is globally generated as a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_U$-modules.
735	\end{definition}
736	
737	\noindent
738	In other words there exists a set $I$ and for
739	each $i$ a section $s_i \in \mathcal{F}(U)$ such
740	that the associated map
741	$$
742	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i \in I} \mathcal{O}_U
743	\longrightarrow
744	\mathcal{F}|_U
745	$$
746	is surjective.
747	
748	\begin{lemma}
749	\label{lemma-pullback-locally-generated}
750	Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
751	be a morphism of ringed spaces.
752	The pullback $f^*\mathcal{G}$ is locally generated by sections
753	if $\mathcal{G}$ is locally generated by sections.
754	\end{lemma}
755	
756	\begin{proof}
757	Given an open subspace $V$ of $Y$ we may
758	consider the commutative diagram of ringed spaces
759	$$
760	\xymatrix{
761	(f^{-1}V, \mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}V}) \ar[r]_{j'} \ar[d]_{f'} &
762	(X, \mathcal{O}_X) \ar[d]^f \\
763	(V, \mathcal{O}_V) \ar[r]^j &
764	(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)
765	}
766	$$
767	We know that $f^*\mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}V} \cong (f')^*(\mathcal{G}|_V)$,
768	see Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-push-pull-composition-modules}.
769	Thus we may assume that $\mathcal{G}$ is globally generated.
770	
771	\medskip\noindent
772	We have seen that $f^*$ commutes with all colimits,
773	and is right exact, see Lemma \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}.
774	Thus if we have a surjection
775	$$
776	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i \in I}
777	\mathcal{O}_Y
778	\to
779	\mathcal{G}
780	\to
781	0
782	$$
783	then upon applying $f^*$ we obtain the surjection
784	$$
785	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i \in I}
786	\mathcal{O}_X
787	\to
788	f^*\mathcal{G}
789	\to
790	0.
791	$$
792	This implies the lemma.
793	\end{proof}
794

806	\section{Modules of finite type}
807	\label{section-finite-type}
808	
809	\begin{definition}
810	\label{definition-finite-type}
811	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
812	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
813	We say that $\mathcal{F}$ is of {\it finite type}
814	if for every $x \in X$ there exists an open
815	neighbourhood $U$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$
816	is generated by finitely many sections.
817	\end{definition}
818	
819	\begin{lemma}
820	\label{lemma-pullback-finite-type}
821	Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
822	be a morphism of ringed spaces.
823	The pullback $f^*\mathcal{G}$ of a finite type
824	$\mathcal{O}_Y$-module is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
825	\end{lemma}
826	
827	\begin{proof}
828	Arguing as in the proof of Lemma \ref{lemma-pullback-locally-generated}
829	we may assume $\mathcal{G}$ is globally generated by finitely
830	many sections.
831	We have seen that $f^*$ commutes with all colimits,
832	and is right exact, see Lemma \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}.
833	Thus if we have a surjection
834	$$
835	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i = 1, \ldots, n}
836	\mathcal{O}_Y
837	\to
838	\mathcal{G}
839	\to
840	0
841	$$
842	then upon applying $f^*$ we obtain the surjection
843	$$
844	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i = 1, \ldots, n}
845	\mathcal{O}_X
846	\to
847	f^*\mathcal{G}
848	\to
849	0.
850	$$
851	This implies the lemma.
852	\end{proof}
853	
854	\begin{lemma}
855	\label{lemma-extension-finite-type}
856	Let $X$ be a ringed space.
857	The image of a morphism of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules of finite
858	type is of finite type.
859	Let
860	$0 \to \mathcal{F}_1 \to \mathcal{F}_2 \to \mathcal{F}_3 \to 0$
861	be a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
862	If $\mathcal{F}_1$ and $\mathcal{F}_3$ are of finite type,
863	so is $\mathcal{F}_2$.
864	\end{lemma}
865	
866	\begin{proof}
867	The statement on images is trivial.
868	The statement on short exact sequences comes from the
869	fact that sections of $\mathcal{F}_3$ locally lift to sections
870	of $\mathcal{F}_2$ and the corresponding result in
871	the category of modules over a ring (applied to the stalks
872	for example).
873	\end{proof}
874	
875	\begin{lemma}
876	\label{lemma-finite-type-surjective-on-stalk}
877	Let $X$ be a ringed space.
878	Let $\varphi : \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{F}$ be a homomorphism
879	of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
880	Let $x \in X$. Assume $\mathcal{F}$ of finite type and
881	the map on stalks
882	$\varphi_x : \mathcal{G}_x \to \mathcal{F}_x$ surjective.
883	Then there exists an open neighbourhood
884	$x \in U \subset X$ such that $\varphi|_U$ is surjective.
885	\end{lemma}
886	
887	\begin{proof}
888	Choose an open neighbourhood $U \subset X$ of $x$ such that $\mathcal{F}$ is
889	generated by $s_1, \ldots, s_n \in \mathcal{F}(U)$ over $U$.
890	By assumption of surjectivity of $\varphi_x$,
891	after shrinking $U$ we may assume that $s_i = \varphi(t_i)$
892	for some $t_i \in \mathcal{G}(U)$.
893	Then $U$ works.
894	\end{proof}
895	
896	\begin{lemma}
897	\label{lemma-finite-type-stalk-zero}
898	Let $X$ be a ringed space.
899	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
900	Let $x \in X$.
901	Assume $\mathcal{F}$ of finite type and $\mathcal{F}_x = 0$.
902	Then there exists an open neighbourhood
903	$x \in U \subset X$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$ is zero.
904	\end{lemma}
905	
906	\begin{proof}
907	This is a special case of
908	Lemma \ref{lemma-finite-type-surjective-on-stalk}
909	applied to the morphism $0 \to \mathcal{F}$.
910	\end{proof}
911	
912	\begin{lemma}
913	\label{lemma-support-finite-type-closed}
914	\begin{slogan}
915	Over any ringed space, sheaves of modules of finite type have closed support.
916	\end{slogan}
917	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
918	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
919	If $\mathcal{F}$ is of finite type then support of $\mathcal{F}$ is closed.
920	\end{lemma}
921	
922	\begin{proof}
923	This is a reformulation of Lemma \ref{lemma-finite-type-stalk-zero}.
924	\end{proof}
925	
926	\begin{lemma}
927	\label{lemma-finite-type-quasi-compact-colimit}
928	Let $X$ be a ringed space. Let $I$ be a preordered set and
929	let $(\mathcal{F}_i, f_{ii'})$ be a system over $I$ consisting of sheaves
930	of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules (see
931	Categories, Section \ref{categories-section-posets-limits}).
932	Let $\mathcal{F} = \colim \mathcal{F}_i$ be the colimit. Assume
933	(a) $I$ is directed,
934	(b) $\mathcal{F}$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, and
935	(c) $X$ is quasi-compact. Then there exists an $i$ such that
936	$\mathcal{F}_i \to \mathcal{F}$ is surjective.
937	If the transition maps $f_{ii'}$ are injective
938	then we conclude that $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{F}_i$ for some $i \in I$.
939	\end{lemma}
940	
941	\begin{proof}
942	Let $x \in X$. There exists an open neighbourhood
943	$U \subset X$ of $x$ and finitely many sections
944	$s_j \in \mathcal{F}(U)$, $j = 1, \ldots, m$ such that
945	$s_1, \ldots, s_m$ generate $\mathcal{F}$ as $\mathcal{O}_U$-module.
946	After possibly shrinking $U$ to a smaller open neighbourhood of $x$
947	we may assume that each $s_j$ comes from a section of $\mathcal{F}_i$
948	for some $i \in I$.
949	Hence, since $X$ is quasi-compact we can find a finite open
950	covering $X = \bigcup_{j = 1, \ldots, m} U_j$, and for each $j$
951	an index $i_j$ and finitely many sections $s_{jl} \in \mathcal{F}_{i_j}(U_j)$
952	whose images generate the restriction of $\mathcal{F}$ to
953	$U_j$. Clearly, the lemma holds for any index $i \in I$ which
954	is $\geq$ all $i_j$.
955	\end{proof}
956	
957	\begin{lemma}
958	\label{lemma-set-isomorphism-classes-finite-type-modules}
959	Let $X$ be a ringed space.
960	There exists a set of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
961	$\{\mathcal{F}_i\}_{i \in I}$ of finite type
962	such that each finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
963	on $X$ is isomorphic to exactly one of the $\mathcal{F}_i$.
964	\end{lemma}
965	
966	\begin{proof}
967	For each open covering $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup U_j$ consider the
968	sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ such that each
969	restriction $\mathcal{F}|_{U_j}$ is a quotient of
970	$\mathcal{O}_{U_j}^{\oplus r_j}$ for some $r_j \geq 0$.
971	These are parametrized by subsheaves
972	$\mathcal{K}_j \subset \mathcal{O}_{U_j}^{\oplus r_j}$ and glueing
973	data
974	$$
975	\varphi_{jj'} :
976	\mathcal{O}_{U_j \cap U_{j'}}^{\oplus r_j}/
977	(\mathcal{K}_j|_{U_j \cap U_{j'}})
978	\longrightarrow
979	\mathcal{O}_{U_j \cap U_{j'}}^{\oplus r_{j'}}/
980	(\mathcal{K}_{j'}|_{U_j \cap U_{j'}})
981	$$
982	see Sheaves, Section \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves}.
983	Note that the collection of all glueing data forms a set.
984	The collection of all coverings $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{j \in J} U_i$
985	where $J \to \mathcal{P}(X)$, $j \mapsto U_j$ is injective forms a set as
986	well. Hence the collection of all sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
987	gotten from glueing quotients as above forms a set $\mathcal{I}$.
988	By definition every finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
989	is isomorphic to an element of $\mathcal{I}$. Choosing an
990	element out of each isomorphism class inside $\mathcal{I}$
991	gives the desired set of sheaves (uses axiom of choice).
992	\end{proof}

1006	\section{Quasi-coherent modules}
1007	\label{section-quasi-coherent}
1008	
1009	\noindent
1010	In this section we introduce an abstract notion of
1011	quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. This notion is very
1012	useful in algebraic geometry, since quasi-coherent modules
1013	on a scheme have a good description on any affine open.
1014	However, we warn the reader that
1015	in the general setting of (locally) ringed spaces
1016	this notion is not well behaved at all. The category of
1017	quasi-coherent sheaves is not abelian in general, infinite
1018	direct sums of quasi-coherent sheaves aren't quasi-coherent, etc, etc.
1019	
1020	\begin{definition}
1021	\label{definition-quasi-coherent}
1022	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1023	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1024	We say that $\mathcal{F}$ is a {\it quasi-coherent
1025	sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules} if for every
1026	point $x \in X$ there exists an open neighbourhood
1027	$x\in U \subset X$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$
1028	is isomorphic to the cokernel of a map
1029	$$
1030	\bigoplus\nolimits_{j \in J}
1031	\mathcal{O}_U
1032	\longrightarrow
1033	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i \in I}
1034	\mathcal{O}_U
1035	$$
1036	The category of quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1037	is denoted $\QCoh(\mathcal{O}_X)$.
1038	\end{definition}
1039	
1040	\noindent
1041	The definition means that $X$ is covered by open sets $U$
1042	such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$ has a {\it presentation}
1043	of the form
1044	$$
1045	\bigoplus\nolimits_{j \in J}
1046	\mathcal{O}_U
1047	\longrightarrow
1048	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i \in I}
1049	\mathcal{O}_U
1050	\longrightarrow
1051	\mathcal{F}|_U
1052	\longrightarrow
1053	0.
1054	$$
1055	Here presentation signifies that the displayed
1056	sequence is exact. In other words
1057	\begin{enumerate}
1058	\item for every point $x$ of $X$ there exists
1059	an open neighbourhood such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$
1060	is generated by global sections, and
1061	\item for a suitable choice of these sections
1062	the kernel of the associated surjection is also
1063	generated by global sections.
1064	\end{enumerate}
1065	
1066	\begin{lemma}
1067	\label{lemma-direct-sum-quasi-coherent}
1068	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1069	The direct sum of two quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules is
1070	a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1071	\end{lemma}
1072	
1073	\begin{proof}
1074	Omitted.
1075	\end{proof}
1076	
1077	\begin{remark}
1078	\label{remark-infinite-direct-sum-quasi-coherent-not}
1079	Warning: It is not true in general that an infinite
1080	direct sum of quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1081	is quasi-coherent. For more esoteric behaviour of quasi-coherent
1082	modules see Example \ref{example-quasi-coherent}.
1083	\end{remark}
1084	
1085	\begin{lemma}
1086	\label{lemma-pullback-quasi-coherent}
1087	Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
1088	be a morphism of ringed spaces.
1089	The pullback $f^*\mathcal{G}$ of a quasi-coherent
1090	$\mathcal{O}_Y$-module is quasi-coherent.
1091	\end{lemma}
1092	
1093	\begin{proof}
1094	Arguing as in the proof of Lemma \ref{lemma-pullback-locally-generated}
1095	we may assume $\mathcal{G}$ has a global presentation by
1096	direct sums of copies of $\mathcal{O}_Y$.
1097	We have seen that $f^*$ commutes with all colimits,
1098	and is right exact, see Lemma \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}.
1099	Thus if we have an exact sequence
1100	$$
1101	\bigoplus\nolimits_{j \in J}
1102	\mathcal{O}_Y
1103	\longrightarrow
1104	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i \in I}
1105	\mathcal{O}_Y
1106	\longrightarrow
1107	\mathcal{G}
1108	\longrightarrow
1109	0
1110	$$
1111	then upon applying $f^*$ we obtain the exact sequence
1112	$$
1113	\bigoplus\nolimits_{j \in J}
1114	\mathcal{O}_X
1115	\longrightarrow
1116	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i \in I}
1117	\mathcal{O}_X
1118	\longrightarrow
1119	f^*\mathcal{G}
1120	\longrightarrow
1121	0.
1122	$$
1123	This implies the lemma.
1124	\end{proof}
1125	
1126	\noindent
1127	This gives plenty of examples of quasi-coherent sheaves.
1128	
1129	\begin{lemma}
1130	\label{lemma-construct-quasi-coherent-sheaves}

1132	Let $\alpha : R \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ring homomorphism from

1147	\bigoplus\nolimits_{j \in J} \mathcal{O}_X

1149	\bigoplus\nolimits_{i \in I} \mathcal{O}_X
1150	\right).
1151	$$

\section{Varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation (the ``d.2'' Mathlib gap)}
\label{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}

This section records the one genuinely Mathlib-absent ingredient behind the
unconditional associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: the commutation of the
stalk functor with the relative tensor product of presheaves of modules over a
\emph{varying} base ring. It is to be built in the new Lean file
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean}. With it the
single left-whiskering obligation of the associator is closed for \emph{all}
modules --- no flatness, no local triviality --- replacing both the flat route
\cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} and the locally-trivial-scoped open route
\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}.

\begin{lemma}
[Stalk of a relative tensor product is the tensor of stalks over the stalk ring]
  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, the tensor-product section
  % --- the stalk of a tensor product of O_X-modules is the tensor product of the
  % stalks over the stalk ring, (F ⊗_{O_X} G)_x = F_x ⊗_{O_{X,x}} G_x. The
  % VERBATIM SOURCE QUOTE could not be retrieved in this session (the reference
  % read channel was unavailable); to be filled from references/stacks-modules.tex
  % before this block is marked complete.
  Let \(X\) be a topological space and let
  \(R : (\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{op} \to \mathtt{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
  commutative rings; write
  \(R' = R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat}\). For
  presheaves of modules \(A, B \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R'\) and a point
  \(x \in X\) there is a natural isomorphism of \(R'.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-modules
  \[
    (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
      \;\cong\;
    A.\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\otimes_{R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x}\; B.\mathtt{stalk}\,x ,
  \]
  where \(\otimes^{p}\) denotes the presheaf-of-modules relative tensor product
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\). This is the ``d.2'' upgrade,
  over a \emph{varying} base ring, of the \(R_x\)-linear stalk map
  (the ``d.1'' \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
  The stalk at \(x\) is the filtered colimit over the neighbourhood filter
  \(\{U \ni x\}\). Sectionwise the relative tensor is computed by
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj\_obj}\),
  \[
    (A \otimes^{p} B)(U) \;=\; A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U) .
  \]
  The tensor product of modules commutes with filtered colimits in each variable,
  and the base ring \(R(U)\) itself colimits to the stalk ring
  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\); combining these,
  \[
    \mathrm{colim}_{U \ni x}\bigl(A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
    \bigl(\mathrm{colim}_{U} A(U)\bigr)
      \otimes_{\mathrm{colim}_{U} R(U)}
    \bigl(\mathrm{colim}_{U} B(U)\bigr)
      \;=\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x .
  \]
  The comparison map is \(R_x\)-linear by the stalk-linearity packaging
  \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, and is a bijection because both sides are the same
  filtered colimit of \(R(U)\)-bilinear data; hence it is an isomorphism of
  \(R_x\)-modules, natural in \(A, B\) and in \(x\). The classical
  fixed-ringed-space special case is the statement
  \((\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})_x = \mathcal{F}_x
  \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X,x}} \mathcal{G}_x\) of the Stacks ``Modules on Ringed
  Spaces'' tensor-product section; here the base ring varies with \(U\), which is
  the genuinely new content.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Left-whiskering a localizer morphism stays in $J.W$, via the stalk tensor]
  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_tensor_commutation, lem:stalk_linear_map}
  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on the small site of a scheme \(X\)
  carrying the locally-bijective localizer \(J.W\)
  (\([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\)). For an \emph{arbitrary}
  presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and any morphism \(g : M \to N\) whose underlying
  additive-presheaf map \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in
  \(J.W\), the left-whisker
  \[
    F \triangleleft g \;=\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
  \]
  is \(J\)-locally injective; indeed it lies in \(J.W\). No flatness and no
  local-triviality hypothesis on \(F\) is required. This unconditional statement
  closes the whiskering-stability obligation that the locally-trivial-scoped
  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} and the sectionwise-flat
  \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} only handled in special cases; it is the single
  obligation consumed by the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation, lem:stalk_linear_map}
  A morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
  isomorphism}: at each \(x \in X\) the induced stalk map \(g_x : M_x \to N_x\) is
  an isomorphism. This is the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) packaging of
  \(J.W\) together with the topological criterion
  \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf.isIso\_iff\_stalkFunctor\_map\_iso}\), and the
  \(R_x\)-linear stalk-map packaging \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}. By the
  stalk--tensor commutation \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}, the stalk of the
  whisker is
  \[
    (F \triangleleft g)_x \;\cong\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x ,
  \]
  i.e.\ the functor \(F_x \otimes_{R_x} (-)\) applied to the isomorphism \(g_x\).
  Functors preserve isomorphisms, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is an isomorphism at
  every \(x\); hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, so it lies in
  \(J.W\) and in particular is locally injective
  (\(\mathtt{GrothendieckTopology.W.isLocallyInjective}\)). No flatness enters,
  because \(g_x\) is \emph{invertible}, not merely injective; tensoring an
  isomorphism by \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) needs no exactness hypothesis on \(F\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}

[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on locally trivial objects]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}

2103|  % is locally free of rank one, when the stalks are local rings)

2105|  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2106|  % "\begin{lemma}
2107|  % \label{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
2108|  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
2109|  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
2110|  % If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
2111|  % the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case).
2112|  % \end{lemma}"
2113|  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M,
2114|  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}: a locally free rank-one
2115|  module is invertible, and over a space whose stalks are local rings (in
2116|  particular any scheme) the converse holds, so on a scheme invertible
2117|  \(\Leftrightarrow\) locally free of rank one --- hence flat.}
2118|  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
2119|  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
2120|  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
2121|  \[
2122|    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2123|      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2124|    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
2125|  \]
2126|  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
2127|  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
2128|  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2129|  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
2130|  coherence is ever required.
2131|\end{lemma}
2132|
2133|\begin{proof}
2134|  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2135|        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
2136|  On a scheme \(X\) every stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) is a local ring, so by Stacks
2137|  tag 0B8M an \(\otimes\)-invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is \emph{locally free
2138|  of rank one}, and a locally free module is in particular \emph{flat}: each
2139|  section \(M(U)\) is a flat \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module. This flatness is the key
2140|  difference from the general-module associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}.
2141|
2142|  \emph{Routing past the open obligation.} The general-module associator
2143|  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is realized through the route-(d) whiskering
2144|  composite, whose left-whiskering half is the flatness-free localizer lemma
2145|  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (the variants
2146|  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\)).
2147|  That half is an \emph{open} obligation, so \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
2148|  currently depends on it. For \emph{invertible} \(M, N, P\) the whiskering objects are
2149|  flat, so the whiskering-stability needed by the associator is instead supplied by
2150|  the \emph{flat}-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (the variants
2151|  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_flat}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_flat}\)),
2152|  which is closed: there local injectivity of \(F \triangleleft g\) follows
2153|  from \(F(U)\) flat over \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) preserving injective linear maps
2154|  (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)), with no open
2155|  residual. Restricting the construction of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to
2156|  invertible arguments therefore bypasses the flatness-free open obligation of
2157|  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}.
2158|
2159|  The remaining ingredients are exactly those of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: the
2160|  three canonical arrows of the local composite are the restriction-compatibility
2161|  comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (twice, on each member of a common
2162|  open cover and in reverse) framing the canonical presheaf-level associator of
2163|  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\). Each arrow is canonical, hence
2164|  natural under further restriction, so the local composites agree on double
2165|  overlaps and glue to the global associator. The invertibility hypotheses are used
2166|  only to route through the flat-whiskering lemma; the gluing itself is unchanged.
2167|\end{proof}

2102|  % modules), Lemma lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1 (an invertible module
2103|  % is locally free of rank one, when the stalks are local rings)
2104|  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
2105|  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2106|  % "\begin{lemma}
2107|  % \label{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
2108|  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
2109|  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
2110|  % If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
2111|  % the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case).
2112|  % \end{lemma}"
2113|  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M,
2114|  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}: a locally free rank-one
2115|  module is invertible, and over a space whose stalks are local rings (in
2116|  particular any scheme) the converse holds, so on a scheme invertible
2117|  \(\Leftrightarrow\) locally free of rank one --- hence flat.}
2118|  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
2119|  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
2120|  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
2121|  \[
2122|    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2123|      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2124|    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
2125|  \]
2126|  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
2127|  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
2128|  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2129|  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
2130|  coherence is ever required.
2131|\end{lemma}
2132|
2133|\begin{proof}
2134|  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2135|        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
2136|  On a scheme \(X\) every stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) is a local ring, so by Stacks
2137|  tag 0B8M an \(\otimes\)-invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is \emph{locally free
2138|  of rank one}, and a locally free module is in particular \emph{flat}: each
2139|  section \(M(U)\) is a flat \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module. This flatness is the key
2140|  difference from the general-module associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}.
2141|
2142|  \emph{Routing past the open obligation.} The general-module associator
2143|  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is realized through the route-(d) whiskering
2144|  composite, whose left-whiskering half is the flatness-free localizer lemma
2145|  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (the variants
2146|  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\)).
2147|  That half is an \emph{open} obligation, so \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
2148|  currently depends on it. For \emph{invertible} \(M, N, P\) the whiskering objects are
2149|  flat, so the whiskering-stability needed by the associator is instead supplied by
2150|  the \emph{flat}-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (the variants
2151|  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_flat}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_flat}\)),
2152|  which is closed: there local injectivity of \(F \triangleleft g\) follows
2153|  from \(F(U)\) flat over \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) preserving injective linear maps
2154|  (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)), with no open
2155|  residual. Restricting the construction of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to
2156|  invertible arguments therefore bypasses the flatness-free open obligation of
2157|  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}.
2158|
2159|  The remaining ingredients are exactly those of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: the
2160|  three canonical arrows of the local composite are the restriction-compatibility
2161|  comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (twice, on each member of a common
2162|  open cover and in reverse) framing the canonical presheaf-level associator of
2163|  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\). Each arrow is canonical, hence
2164|  natural under further restriction, so the local composites agree on double
2165|  overlaps and glue to the global associator. The invertibility hypotheses are used
2166|  only to route through the flat-whiskering lemma; the gluing itself is unchanged.
2167|\end{proof}

content:
\section{Tensor product}
\label{section-tensor-product}

\noindent
We have already briefly discussed the tensor product in the setting
of change of rings in Sheaves, Sections
\ref{sheaves-section-presheaves-modules} and
\ref{sheaves-section-sheafification-presheaves-modules}.
Let us generalize this to tensor products of modules.

\medskip\noindent
Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
$\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
$$
\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
$$
as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
$\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
$\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
as the sheafification of the above:
$$
\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
=
(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
$$
This can be characterized as the sheaf of
$\mathcal{O}_X$-modules such that for any third
sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{H}$
we have
$$
\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}
(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H})
=
\text{Bilin}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F} \times \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}).
$$
Here the right hand side indicates the set of bilinear maps of sheaves
of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules as defined in Section \ref{section-bilinear}.

\medskip\noindent
The tensor product of modules $M, N$ over a ring $R$ satisfies symmetry,
namely $M \otimes_R N = N \otimes_R M$, hence the same holds for
tensor products of sheaves of modules, i.e., we have
$$
\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
=
\mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
$$
functorial in $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$.
And since tensor product of modules satisfies associativity we
also get canonical functorial isomorphisms
$$
(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
\otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{H}
=
\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
(\mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{H})
$$
functorial in $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$, and $\mathcal{H}$.

\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}
Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
Let $x \in X$. There is a canonical isomorphism
of $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-modules
$$
(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})_x
=
\mathcal{F}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}} \mathcal{G}_x
$$
functorial in $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Omitted.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-tensor-product-sheafification}
Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
Let $\mathcal{F}'$, $\mathcal{G}'$ be presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
with sheafifications $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$. Then
$\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} =
(\mathcal{F}' \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}')^\#$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Omitted.
\end{proof}


\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-tensor-product-exact}
Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
Let $\mathcal{G}$ be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
If
$\mathcal{F}_1
\to \mathcal{F}_2
\to \mathcal{F}_3
\to 0$
is an exact sequence of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules then
the induced sequence
$$
\mathcal{F}_1 \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
\mathcal{F}_2 \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
\mathcal{F}_3 \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
0
$$
is exact.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
This follows from the fact that exactness may be checked at stalks
(Lemma \ref{lemma-abelian}), the description of stalks
(Lemma \ref{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}) and the corresponding
result for tensor products of modules
(Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-tensor-product-exact}).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}
Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be
a morphism of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$
be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules. Then
$f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{G})
= f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{G}$
functorially in $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$.
\end{lemma}
